title: Presigned Browser Uploads, POST Policies, and CORS · Stakeholder Go Mission
summary: A state civil-rights evidence office. They need bounded accessible direct uploads, generated keys, integrity, quarantine, malware-scan handoff, presigned browser policy, CORS, finalization, and private retrieval without confusing browser success with publication. Planned artifact: Deliver Accessible Evidence Upload Portal with acceptance evidence, rubric defense, reflection, and a changed-constraint extension. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal…
objectives: Enforce per-file, request, tenant, concurrent, time, and aggregate byte budgets before and during transfer.
Model admitted, uploading, quarantined, scanning, accepted, rejected, published, and deleted transitions with authorized owners.
Create a one-object upload intent with tenant, generated key, expected size, type, checksum, expiry, status, and replay policy before signing.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and Go Commit to the mission model · Project Upload Byte Time Count Budget
steps.instruction:
project case — A project Go file-delivery team handles case 9205 during a compliance restore drill. Build deterministic Go evidence to finalize a direct upload against intent while separating CORS from IAM authorization; preserve accessible progress, cancellation, status, and recovery evidence; then introduce a duplicate event or ambiguous response and reconcile without duplicate effect. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, databases, credentials, host state, race and leak checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: S3, CloudFront, and Go Commit to the mission model · Project Upload Byte Time Count Budget.
